edges.description: Reduced G6PD activity limits pentose-phosphate shunt flux and NADPH generation.
Severe variants can cause chronic hemolysis rather than only trigger-induced acute crises.
Reduced G6PD activity in newborns can produce neonatal hyperbilirubinemia, with bilirubin accumulation presenting as neonatal jaundice.
intermediate_mechanisms: bilirubin accumulation during the neonatal period

edges.description: Inadequate NADPH and glutathione buffering amplify oxidative injury in erythrocytes.
Oxidant stress modifies hemoglobin and erythrocyte proteins when antioxidant buffering is insufficient.
Oxidative hemoglobin precipitation and membrane injury promote erythrocyte destruction.
Erythrocyte destruction produces acute hemolytic anemia.
When fava-bean oxidants are the precipitating trigger, acute hemolytic anemia presents clinically as favism.
Intravascular hemolysis can produce hemoglobinuria or dark red urine.
Acute anemia decreases circulating red-cell mass and produces pallor.
Increased heme breakdown raises bilirubin and produces jaundice.
Acute anemia can cause systemic fatigue or malaise.
Severe acute anemia can cause dyspnea or respiratory distress.
Extravascular erythrocyte clearance can enlarge or stress the spleen during favism episodes.

edges.description: Low measured G6PD activity reports the causal enzymatic defect.
Bilirubin elevation reflects heme breakdown during hemolysis.
Increased bilirubin is the biochemical basis of neonatal hyperbilirubinemia.
Heinz bodies indicate oxidized hemoglobin precipitation in erythrocytes.
Impaired glutathione homeostasis reports deficient NADPH-dependent antioxidant buffering.

nodes.description: Oxidative stressors trigger episodic erythrocyte destruction and anemia in susceptible G6PD-deficient individuals.

evidence.reference_title: G6PD deficiency: An update.
evidence.snippet: The G6PD enzyme is critical to protecting erythrocytes against oxidative stress, and deficiency may lead to hemolysis in the presence of certain environmental factors such as infection and some medications and foods.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency: An Actionable Risk Factor for Patients with COVID-19?
evidence.snippet: Although most individuals are asymptomatic, exposure to certain food, drugs, or infections can trigger acute hemolytic anemia.

nodes.description: Identifying and avoiding medications, foods, infections, and other oxidant triggers that can precipitate hemolysis.

evidence.reference_title: G6PD deficiency: An update.
evidence.snippet: By recognizing the potential for G6PD deficiency, clinicians can screen for the disorder and teach affected patients how to avoid triggers that result in harmful clinical manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Perioperative management of the glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficient patient: a review of literature.
evidence.snippet: The most effective management strategy is to prevent hemolysis by avoiding oxidative stressors.

evidence.reference_title: Favism: Clinical Features at Different Ages.
evidence.snippet: Laboratory findings are characterized by anemia, reticulocytosis, elevated bilirubin level, and sometimes urinary urobilinogen and methemoglobinemia.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency in Greek newborns: the Mediterranean C563T mutation screening.
evidence.snippet: G6PD activity showed statistically significant correlation with total bilirubin blood levels.

readouts.interpretation: Bilirubin elevation reflects heme breakdown during hemolysis.
Increased bilirubin is the biochemical basis of neonatal hyperbilirubinemia.

nodes.description: Restrictive red-cell transfusion is used for severe acute hemolytic crisis with very low hemoglobin or clinical instability.
nodes.id: Blood Transfusion During Severe Acute Hemolysis

evidence.reference_title: Management of children with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency presenting with acute haemolytic crisis during the SARs-COV-2 pandemic.
evidence.snippet: 33% of patients had Hb < 50 g/L and were all transfused. 50% had Hb between 50 and 65 g/L, half of them (n = 49) did not receive transfusion and only two patients (4%) required transfusion upon follow up.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of G6PD deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Acute hemolysis is self-limited, but in rare instances it can be severe enough to warrant a blood transfusion.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase--from oxidative stress to cellular functions and degenerative diseases.
evidence.snippet: deficiency is associated with neonatal jaundice, drug- or infection-mediated hemolytic crisis, favism and, less commonly, chronic non-spherocytic hemolytic anemia

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of G6PD deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The variant that causes chronic hemolysis is uncommon because it is related to sporadic gene mutation rather than the more common inherited gene mutation.

evidence.reference_title: Study of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency: 5 Years Retrospective Egyptian Study.
evidence.snippet: Patients presented mainly with pallor (1000 patients; 100%), dark red urine (896 patients; 89.6%) and jaundice (878 patients; 87.8%) after 24-72 hours of exposure to the precipitating factors

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase variants associated with favism in Thai children.
evidence.snippet: The classic features of favism, which are pallor, hemoglobinuria, and jaundice, were detected in all cases.

nodes.description: Pathogenic variants in G6PD reduce enzyme stability or activity in erythrocytes, limiting the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: G6PD deficiency, one of the commonest inherited enzyme abnormalities in humans, arises through one of many possible mutations, most of which reduce the stability of the enzyme and its level as red cells age.

evidence.reference_title: A novel G6PD deleterious variant identified in three families with severe glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Infants with this novel variant showed decreased activity of G6PD, severe anemia, and pathological jaundice

evidence.reference_title: Favism: Clinical Features at Different Ages.
evidence.snippet: Some other symptoms were present in all: jaundice, increased bilirubin, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, discolored urine, tachycardia, pallor, abdominal pain, malaise, vomit, nausea, and dizziness.

evidence.reference_title: Favism: Clinical Features at Different Ages.
evidence.snippet: Favism is a hemolytic disease due to the ingestion of fava beans in individuals with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: any of them may develop acute and sometimes very severe hemolytic anemia when triggered by ingestion of fava beans

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is 1 of the commonest human enzymopathies, caused by inherited mutations of the X-linked gene G6PD.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency in Greek newborns: the Mediterranean C563T mutation screening.
evidence.snippet: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) gene is located at the X-chromosome at Xq28 and the disease is recessively inherited predominantly in males.

evidence.reference_title: G6PD / G6PD deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: G6PD | HGNC:4057 | G6PD deficiency | MONDO:0005775 | XL | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: Inherited, non-spherocytic haemolysis due to deficiency of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: The laboratory investigation of G6PD deficiency is commonly done by a quantitative spectrophotometric analysis or by a rapid fluorescent spot test detecting the generation of NADPH from NADP.

evidence.reference_title: A novel G6PD deleterious variant identified in three families with severe glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Infants with this novel variant showed decreased activity of G6PD, severe anemia, and pathological jaundice

readouts.interpretation: Low measured G6PD activity reports the causal enzymatic defect.

evidence.reference_title: Donor glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency decreases blood quality for transfusion.
evidence.snippet: As expected, fresh G6PD-deficient RBCs demonstrated defects in the oxidative phase of the pentose phosphate pathway. During refrigerated storage, G6PD-deficient RBCs demonstrated increased glycolysis, impaired glutathione homeostasis, and increased purine oxidation, as compared with G6PD-normal RBCs.

readouts.interpretation: Impaired glutathione homeostasis reports deficient NADPH-dependent antioxidant buffering.

evidence.reference_title: Inherited, non-spherocytic haemolysis due to deficiency of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: The presence of unopposed oxidizing agents leading to oxidation of the sulfhydryl bridges between parts of the haemoglobin molecule decrease the solubility of haemoglobin, leading to precipitations called Heinz bodies.

readouts.interpretation: Heinz bodies indicate oxidized hemoglobin precipitation in erythrocytes.

evidence.reference_title: G6PD deficiency: the genotype-phenotype association.
evidence.snippet: People, usually males, with deficient alleles are susceptible to neonatal jaundice, and acute hemolytic anemia, usually during infection, after treatment with certain drugs or after eating fava beans.

evidence.reference_title: Study of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency: 5 Years Retrospective Egyptian Study.
evidence.snippet: G6PD deficiency anemia presented mainly with pallor, dark red urine and jaundice after exposure to certain diets, drugs and diseases

nodes.description: Reduced G6PD activity lowers NADPH availability and compromises glutathione-dependent antioxidant buffering.

evidence.reference_title: [Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase].
evidence.snippet: Glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) plays a key role in the generation of NADPH which is essential for maintaining glutathione in the reduce state

evidence.reference_title: The Redox Role of G6PD in Cell Growth, Cell Death, and Cancer.
evidence.snippet: The generation of reducing equivalent NADPH via glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) is critical for the maintenance of redox homeostasis and reductive biosynthesis in cells.

evidence.reference_title: Study of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency: 5 Years Retrospective Egyptian Study.
evidence.snippet: Patients presented mainly with pallor (1000 patients; 100%), dark red urine (896 patients; 89.6%) and jaundice (878 patients; 87.8%) after 24-72 hours of exposure to the precipitating factors

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase variants associated with favism in Thai children.
evidence.snippet: The classic features of favism, which are pallor, hemoglobinuria, and jaundice, were detected in all cases.

nodes.description: Phototherapy or exchange transfusion may be required to prevent kernicterus in G6PD-associated neonatal hyperbilirubinemia.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of G6PD deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Neonatal hyperbilirubinemia may require treatment with phototherapy or exchange transfusion to prevent kernicterus.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: G6PD-deficient persons are mostly asymptomatic, but they can develop severe jaundice during the neonatal period and acute hemolytic anemia when they ingest fava beans or when they are exposed to certain infections or drugs.

evidence.reference_title: The global prevalence of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: This inherited deficiency causes neonatal hyperbilirubinemia and chronic hemolytic anemia.

nodes.description: Early detection and close monitoring identify G6PD-deficient newborns at risk for severe neonatal jaundice.

evidence.reference_title: National G6PD neonatal screening program in Gaza Strip of Palestine: rationale, challenges and recommendations.
evidence.snippet: Early detection and an accurate diagnosis are, therefore, of major importance for preventing negative patient outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency in girls: a diagnosis not to be missed (a case report).
evidence.snippet: Through this observation, we insist on the importance of neonatal screening in regions with a high prevalence of hemolysis in order to avoid diagnostic delays and also to prioritize the evaluation to be requested in an acute hemolysis state, to propose an education articulated around a preventive approach in children with this disease.

nodes.description: Unopposed oxidants damage hemoglobin sulfhydryl groups, reduce hemoglobin solubility, and form Heinz body precipitates.

evidence.reference_title: Inherited, non-spherocytic haemolysis due to deficiency of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: The presence of unopposed oxidizing agents leading to oxidation of the sulfhydryl bridges between parts of the haemoglobin molecule decrease the solubility of haemoglobin, leading to precipitations called Heinz bodies.

nodes.description: NADPH and glutathione insufficiency leave G6PD-deficient erythrocytes susceptible to oxidant injury from drugs, infections, foods, or chemicals.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase--from oxidative stress to cellular functions and degenerative diseases.
evidence.snippet: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD), the first and rate-limiting enzyme of the pentose phosphate pathway, is indispensable to maintenance of the cytosolic pool of NADPH and thus the cellular redox balance.

evidence.reference_title: Periodontal disease and hemolysis in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency: Is there a nexus?
evidence.snippet: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is an erythrocyte enzyme defect that amplifies the susceptibility of erythrocytes to oxidative stress due to excessive levels of reactive oxygen species. Consequently, erythrocyte destruction and hemolysis occur.

evidence.reference_title: Study of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency: 5 Years Retrospective Egyptian Study.
evidence.snippet: Patients presented mainly with pallor (1000 patients; 100%), dark red urine (896 patients; 89.6%) and jaundice (878 patients; 87.8%) after 24-72 hours of exposure to the precipitating factors

evidence.reference_title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency: the added value of cytology.
evidence.snippet: The evolution was marked by a respiratory distress syndrome, a severe hemolytic anemia, associated with thrombocytopenia and kidney failure.

evidence.reference_title: Favism: Clinical Features at Different Ages.
evidence.snippet: Some other symptoms were present in all: jaundice, increased bilirubin, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, discolored urine, tachycardia, pallor, abdominal pain, malaise, vomit, nausea, and dizziness.
